perturbation.original_text: The term “Confidential Information” as used herein means all nonpublic information relating to the Subject Matter that is disclosed by either party, its Affiliates (as defined below), or their agents (where applicable, collectively referred to as the “Disclosing Party”) , directly or indirectly, in writin g, orally or by inspection of premises or tangible objects to the other party (the “ Recipient ”) that is: (i) marked confidential or proprietary, or (ii) given the nature of the information or the circumstances surrounding its disclosure, reasonably should be deemed confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “Confidential Information” as used herein means all information, whether public or nonpublic, relating to the Subject Matter that is disclosed by either party, its Affiliates (as defined below), or their agents (where applicable, collectively referred to as the “Disclosing Party”) , directly or indirectly, in writin g, orally or by inspection of premises or tangible objects to the other party (the “ Recipient ”).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'nonpublic' qualification, the definition of Confidential Information now includes public information, which contradicts the common understanding and legal definition of confidential information or trade secrets. This introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification redefines "Confidential Information" to include public information. The legal definition of a trade secret and other forms of confidential information under 18 U.S. Code § 1839 explicitly requires the information to be secret and not generally known or readily ascertainable. Including public information nullifies the protection afforded to legitimately confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines "Confidential Information" to include public information, which directly contradicts the established legal understanding and definition of what constitutes confidential information. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, implies that trade secrets involve elements of secrecy which is inconsistent with public information. The changed contract language broadens the definition of Confidential Information to include publicly available data, diminishing the protections and remedies available for legitimately confidential information under the law. This alteration creates a legal contradiction because it attempts to protect information that, by its nature, does not qualify for protection.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Confidential Information as it uses for its own confidential, proprietary and trade secret information, but in no case us e less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall use a minimal degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the required degree of care to 'minimal' directly contradicts the standard of 'reasonable care' typically required to protect confidential information and trade secrets. This lowers the bar for protection and could be seen as a breach of duty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets to be protected. Stipulating 'minimal' care contradicts the 'reasonable efforts' standard, potentially stripping the information of trade secret status because a trade secret requires some effort to protect it.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'reasonable care,' aligning with the UTSA's standard of 'reasonable efforts' to protect trade secrets. The modified contract specifies 'minimal care,' which directly contradicts the reasonable efforts standard necessary to maintain trade secret status. This contradiction falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the changed language deviates from the legally recognized standard. The scraped snippets were not helpful.

perturbation.original_text: Confidentiality obligations shall survive termination of this Agreement for the Period of Confidentiality set forth above unless the Confidential Information is a trade secret, in which case the confidentiality obligations shall continue for as long as the information is a trade secret.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidentiality obligations shall terminate immediately upon termination of this Agreement, regardless of whether the Confidential Information is a trade secret.
perturbation.explanation: This modification eliminates the perpetual protection afforded to trade secrets after the agreement terminates. Trade secrets, by law, are intended to be protected indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. Limiting confidentiality obligations to the term of the agreement, even for trade secrets, undermines this protection by allowing misappropriation once the agreement ends, conflicting with the indefinite protection trade secrets are meant to have as long as they maintain their secret status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1831 - Economic espionage | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1831 - Economic espionage U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— Whoever, intending or knowing that the offense will benefit any foreign government, foreign instrumentality , or foreign agent, knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains a trade secret ; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys a trade secret ; (3) receives, buys, or possesses a trade secret , knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in any of paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in any of paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined not more than $5,000,000 or imprisoned not more than 15 years, or both. (b) Organizations.— Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $10,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3488 ; amended Pub. L. 112–269, § 2 , Jan. 14, 2013 , 126 Stat. 2442 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2013—Subsec. (a). Pub. L. 112–269, § 2(a) , substituted “not more than $5,000,000” for “not more than $500,000” in concluding provisions. Subsec. (b). Pub. L. 112–2

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the protection of trade secrets as it eliminates the perpetual protection afforded to trade secrets after the agreement terminates, regardless of whether the Confidential Information is a trade secret. The original text protects trade secrets indefinitely if they remain a trade secret, while the modified text terminates confidentiality obligations immediately upon termination of the agreement. This conflicts with the Economic Espionage Act, as described in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which outlines penalties for misappropriating trade secrets. Although Snippet 1 focuses on economic espionage benefiting a foreign entity, it underscores the legal significance and protection afforded to trade secrets, which the modified text undermines by failing to provide indefinite protection. The second snippet is a 404 error.

perturbation.original_text: If Recipient is requested , ordered or required by a regulatory agency or any other government authority or a court to disclose any Confidential Information, Recipient shall promptly notify Disclosing Party of such request , order or requirement so that Disclosing Part y may have the opportunity to contest the disclosure, including seek ing a protective order , or waive Recipient’s compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If Recipient is requested, ordered, or required by a regulatory agency or any other government authority or a court to disclose any Confidential Information, Recipient shall disclose such information without notifying the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the Recipient to notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing Confidential Information as mandated by a legal or regulatory body directly contradicts the principles of protecting trade secrets and confidential information. The modification prevents the Disclosing Party from taking legal action to protect their information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 26(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 26(c) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure allows parties to seek protective orders to protect confidential information in discovery. By mandating disclosure without notice, the Recipient is actively preventing the Disclosing Party from seeking such a protective order, thus undermining the legal safeguards in place for confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the Recipient's obligation to notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing confidential information, which directly undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to seek protective orders under Rule 26(c) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. The Law Explanation states that the Recipient is actively preventing the Disclosing Party from seeking such a protective order. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Rule 26, which governs discovery and disclosures, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 generally discusses the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure governing civil proceedings. While neither snippet explicitly mentions a notification requirement, the Law Explanation clarifies that removing the notification directly prevents the Disclosing Party from leveraging Rule 26(c) to protect confidential information, thus creating a legal contradiction according to the Misaligned Terminology definition.
